output.answer:
## Long-Running Issues With No Meaningful Progress, January–April 2026

Three issues stand out as genuinely stuck — not just slow, but showing no substantive movement across the entire four-month window.

---

### 1. Nevada DMV Holds on Parking Violations

**What is stuck:** The city cannot place holds on Nevada-registered vehicles with unpaid parking tickets, which directly limits how much revenue it can recover. This problem was first flagged in JAN-003 (January 11), noted again in FEB-018 (February 24), and still unresolved as of APR-004 (April 14) — a span of at least five months. Every parking update memo describes the same status: "pending state Department of Public Safety action." No deadline has been set, no escalation to state officials is documented, and no workaround has been implemented.

**Why it matters across memos:** The city has collected $633K in parking revenue since June 2025 (APR-004), but the memos suggest that figure is constrained by the inability to enforce against Nevada plates. California DMV access was successfully obtained in January (FEB-018), which makes the Nevada stall more conspicuous — the city solved the out-of-state problem faster than the in-state one.

---

### 2. Seniors EngAGED ARPA Funding Cliff

**What is stuck:** The ARPA-funded senior programming ends in December 2026, and no successor funding has been identified. JAN-008 (January 21) flagged this as an open action item. APR-010 (April 21) — three months later — reports exactly the same status: "ARPA funding concludes Dec 2026; no extension funding identified." The language is nearly identical across both memos, which is itself a signal that nothing has moved.

**Why it matters across memos:** Participation actually *declined* between the two reporting periods — from 1,720 participants in Q4 2025 (JAN-008) to 1,256 in Q1 2026 (APR-010). The memos do not explain the drop, but it adds urgency: the program is shrinking while its funding runway is also shrinking, and no plan exists to address either trend.

---

### 3. Boards and Commissions Persistent Vacancies

**What is stuck:** Several boards have carried the same vacancies from January through April with no documented appointments. The Access Advisory Board had 6 vacancies in JAN-002 (January 11) and still had 6 vacancies in APR-013 (April 22). Ward 6 NAB had 6 vacancies in JAN-002 and still had 6 in APR-013. The Senior Residents Advisory Board had 4 vacancies in both reports.

**Why it matters across memos:** JAN-002 noted that staff planned to meet individually with Council members to support timely appointments — but APR-013 shows no reduction in vacancies on the most affected boards. The memos do not confirm whether those meetings happened. This is a governance gap: boards that cannot reach quorum cannot conduct business, and the pattern suggests the recruitment process is not working.

---

### What These Three Have in Common

All three are blocked by factors outside the immediate control of the departments writing the memos — state-level bureaucracy (DMV holds), a funding cliff with no identified alternative (ARPA), and a Council appointment process that appears stalled (vacancies). In each case, the memos flag the problem repeatedly but document no escalation, no deadline, and no change in status. That combination — repeated flagging, zero movement, no accountability mechanism visible in the record — is what distinguishes these from issues that are simply slow.
